title: CTM-AI: A Blueprint for General AI Inspired by a Model of Consciousness
summary: Despite remarkable advances, today's AI systems remain narrow in scope, falling short of the flexible, adaptive, and multisensory intelligence that characterizes human capabilities. This gap has fueled longstanding debates about whether AI might one day achieve human-like generality or even consciousness, and whether theories of consciousness can inspire new architectures for AI. This paper presents an early blueprint for implementing a general AI system, CTM-AI, combining the Conscious Turing Machine (CTM), a formal machine model of consciousness, with today's foundation models. CTM-AI contains an enormous number of powerful processors ranging from specialized experts (e.g., vision-language models and APIs) to unspecialized general-purpose learners poised to develop their own expertise. Crucially, for whatever problem must be dealt with, information from many processors is selected, integrated, and exchanged appropriately to solve the task. CTM-AI achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on MUStARD (72.28) and UR-FUNNY (72.13), outperforming multimodal and multi-agent frameworks. On tool-using and agentic tasks, CTM-AI achieves 10+ points of improvement on StableToolBench and WebArena-Lite. Overall, CTM-AI offers a principled, testable blueprint for general AI inspired by a model of consciousness.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes CTM-AI, a general AI system that combines the Conscious Turing Machine (a formal model of consciousness) with modern foundation models. CTM-AI uses many specialized and general-purpose processors with selective information integration, achieving strong results on multimodal (MUStARD, UR-FUNNY) and tool-using benchmarks (StableToolBench, WebArena-Lite).
AI.motivation: Current AI systems are narrow and lack the flexible, adaptive, multisensory intelligence of humans. There is also a gap between theories of consciousness and practical AI architectures. The authors aim to bridge these gaps by creating a general AI system inspired by a formal consciousness model.
AI.method: CTM-AI combines the Conscious Turing Machine (CTM) framework with today's foundation models. It employs a massive number of processors, ranging from specialized experts (vision-language models, APIs) to unspecialized general-purpose learners. Information from many processors is selected, integrated, and exchanged appropriately to solve tasks.
AI.result: CTM-AI achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on MUStARD (72.28) and UR-FUNNY (72.13), outperforming multimodal and multi-agent frameworks. On tool-using and agentic tasks, it achieves 10+ points of improvement on StableToolBench and WebArena-Lite.
AI.conclusion: CTM-AI offers a principled, testable blueprint for general AI inspired by a model of consciousness, demonstrating that formal consciousness theories can inform practical AI system design.

title: ProtDBench: A Unified Benchmark of Protein Binder Design and Evaluation
summary: Recent advances in de novo protein binder design have enabled increasing experimental validation, yet reported in silico metrics remain difficult to interpret or compare across studies due to non-standardized evaluation protocols. We introduce ProtDBench, a standardized and throughput-aware evaluation framework for protein binder design. ProtDBench defines unified benchmark tasks, evaluation protocols, and success criteria, enabling systematic analysis of how evaluation design influences observed performance. Using a large wet-lab annotated dataset, we analyze commonly used structure prediction models as evaluation verifiers, revealing substantial verifier-dependent bias and limited agreement under identical filtering protocols. We then benchmark representative open-source generative binder design methods across ten diverse protein targets under a fixed evaluation protocol. Beyond per-sequence success rates, ProtDBench incorporates throughput-aware metrics based on a fixed 24-hour budget, as well as cluster-level success criteria to account for structural diversity. Together, these results expose systematic differences induced by filtering rules, success definitions, and throughput-aware evaluation between computational efficiency, success rate, and structural diversity. Overall, ProtDBench provides a fair and reproducible evaluation pipeline that supports systematic and controlled comparison of protein binder design methods under realistic evaluation settings.

AI.tldr: ProtDBench is a standardized evaluation framework for protein binder design that reveals how different evaluation protocols (verifiers, filtering rules, success criteria, throughput awareness) systematically bias performance metrics, using wet-lab data across ten protein targets.
AI.motivation: Current protein binder design evaluations lack standardized protocols, making it difficult to compare methods across studies due to inconsistent metrics and verifier choices.
AI.method: ProtDBench defines unified benchmarks, evaluation protocols, and success criteria; analyzes structure prediction verifiers for bias; benchmarks generative methods across ten targets; incorporates throughput-aware metrics (24-hour budget) and cluster-level success criteria for structural diversity.
AI.result: Substantial verifier-dependent bias and limited agreement found under identical filtering protocols. Systematic differences exposed by filtering rules, success definitions, and throughput-aware evaluation between computational efficiency, success rate, and structural diversity.
AI.conclusion: ProtDBench enables fair, reproducible, and systematic comparison of protein binder design methods under realistic settings.

title: Tree-Conditioned Edit Flows for Ancestral Sequence Reconstruction
comment: 9 pages of main text, 3 figures, 3 tables, and 1 algorithm. This version is a preliminary preprint
summary: Ancestral sequence reconstruction (ASR) aims to infer extinct protein sequences at internal nodes of a phylogenetic tree. Classical ASR methods are typically based on continuous-time Markov substitution models, but they treat sites largely independently and handle insertions and deletions only weakly or not at all. We introduce a tree-conditioned edit-flow model for variable-length ASR. Given two descendant sequences and their branch distances to a shared ancestor, the model reconstructs the ancestor through paired bidirectional edit trajectories constrained to agree on a common ancestral state. On a benchmark of experimentally evolved sequences with only context-independent substitutions, the model does not match the accuracy of the best classical method, yet still achieves reasonable performance despite being trained on natural sequences that include insertions, deletions, and substitutions. On a benchmark of natural homologous sequences with abundant insertions and deletions, the model most accurately localizes inferred evolutionary change.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a tree-conditioned edit-flow model for variable-length ancestral sequence reconstruction that handles insertions and deletions, performing reasonably on substitution-only benchmarks and achieving best accuracy on natural sequences with indels.
AI.motivation: Classical ASR methods treat sites independently and handle insertions/deletions poorly or not at all, motivating the need for a model that can reconstruct variable-length ancestral sequences with indels.
AI.method: A tree-conditioned edit-flow model that reconstructs ancestors through paired bidirectional edit trajectories from two descendant sequences, constrained to agree on a common ancestral state.
AI.result: The model doesn't match classical methods on substitution-only benchmarks but achieves reasonable performance; on natural homologous sequences with indels, it most accurately localizes inferred evolutionary change.
AI.conclusion: The proposed model improves ASR for sequences with insertions and deletions, where classical methods are limited.

title: A foundation model of vision, audition, and language for in-silico neuroscience
summary: Cognitive neuroscience is fragmented into specialized models, each tailored to specific experimental paradigms, hence preventing a unified model of cognition in the human brain. Here, we introduce TRIBE v2, a tri-modal (video, audio and language) foundation model capable of predicting human brain activity in a variety of naturalistic and experimental conditions. Leveraging a unified dataset of over 1,000 hours of fMRI across 720 subjects, we demonstrate that our model accurately predicts high-resolution brain responses for novel stimuli, tasks and subjects, superseding traditional linear encoding models, delivering several-fold improvements in accuracy. Critically, TRIBE v2 enables in silico experimentation: tested on seminal visual and neuro-linguistic paradigms, it recovers a variety of results established by decades of empirical research. Finally, by extracting interpretable latent features, TRIBE v2 reveals the fine-grained topography of multisensory integration. These results establish artificial intelligence as a unifying framework for exploring the functional organization of the human brain.

AI.tldr: TRIBE v2 is a tri-modal foundation model (video, audio, language) trained on 1,000+ hours of fMRI data from 720 subjects that predicts brain activity across naturalistic and experimental conditions, outperforming traditional linear models and enabling in silico replication of established neuroscientific findings.
AI.motivation: Cognitive neuroscience lacks a unified model of cognition, being fragmented into specialized models for specific experimental paradigms.
AI.method: TRIBE v2 is a tri-modal (video, audio, language) foundation model trained on a large-scale unified fMRI dataset (1,000+ hours, 720 subjects) to predict brain activity.
AI.result: Outperforms traditional linear encoding models with several-fold accuracy improvements; recovers established findings from visual and neuro-linguistic paradigms in silico; reveals fine-grained topography of multisensory integration via interpretable latent features.
AI.conclusion: AI foundation models can serve as a unifying framework for exploring the functional organization of the human brain.

title: Dissociating spatial frequency reliance from adversarial robustness advantages in neurally guided deep convolutional neural networks
summary: Deep convolutional neural networks (DCNNs) have rivaled humans on many visual tasks, yet they remain vulnerable to near-imperceptible perturbations generated by adversarial attacks. Recent work shows that aligning DCNN representations with human visual cortex activity improves adversarial robustness, but the mechanisms driving this advantage are unclear. One hypothesis suggests that neural alignment confers robustness by biasing models away from brittle high-frequency details and towards the low spatial frequencies (LSF). However, recent work shows that human object recognition critically depends on a narrow, mid-frequency "human channel". Interestingly, this band was partially preserved in prior LSF-focused studies. Here, we investigate whether a spectral bias towards the LSF or the human channel is the primary driver of the adversarial robustness observed in neurally aligned DCNNs. We first show that DCNNs aligned to higher-order regions of the human ventral visual stream systematically increase reliance on both LSF and the human channel. However, directly steering DCNNs towards these bands revealed a clear dissociation. Biasing models towards the human channel, either alone or together with LSF, does not improve robustness and even impairs it. LSF bias produced some robustness gains, but such improvements are modest despite inducing much larger shifts in spatial-frequency reliance than neurally aligned models. Spatial-frequency-biased models overall show little, if any, increase in similarity to human neural representational geometry. Together, our results suggest that altered spatial-frequency reliance is likely an emergent property of learning more human-like representations rather than the primary mechanism by which neural alignment confers adversarial robustness, and motivate the need for future research examining representational properties beyond spatial-frequency profiles.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates why aligning DCNNs with human visual cortex improves adversarial robustness. The authors test whether robustness comes from bias toward low spatial frequencies (LSF) or the mid-frequency "human channel." They find that neural alignment increases reliance on both bands, but directly steering models toward the human channel does not improve robustness (and can impair it), while LSF bias yields only modest gains. The results suggest altered spatial-frequency reliance is an emergent property of neural alignment, not the primary mechanism for adversarial robustness.
AI.motivation: To understand the mechanisms by which aligning DCNN representations with human visual cortex activity improves adversarial robustness, specifically testing whether spatial-frequency bias (low spatial frequencies or the human mid-frequency channel) is the primary driver.
AI.method: The authors systematically compared neurally aligned DCNNs (aligned to higher-order ventral visual stream regions) against models directly biased toward LSF, the human channel, or both, evaluating their adversarial robustness and spatial-frequency reliance.
AI.result: Neural alignment increased reliance on both LSF and the human channel, but directly biasing models toward the human channel did not improve robustness (even impaired it), and LSF bias yielded only modest gains. Spatial-frequency-biased models showed little similarity to human neural representational geometry.
AI.conclusion: Altered spatial-frequency reliance is likely an emergent property of learning human-like representations rather than the primary mechanism by which neural alignment confers adversarial robustness. Future research should examine representational properties beyond spatial-frequency profiles.

title: A Generalized Framework of Antisymmetric Polyspectral Indices for Identifying High-Order Neural Interactions
summary: Cross-frequency interactions are fundamental brain mechanisms for integrating information across temporal scales. However, accurate identification of these couplings is hindered by complex multi-frequency nonlinearities and by spurious, zero-lag artifacts caused by volume conduction. To our knowledge, conventional metrics lack a robust framework to characterize genuine interactions among multiple time series where a frequency of interest $f_N$ arises from the combination of $N-1$ components such that $f_N = \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} f_i$. We introduce a general family of antisymmetric cross-polyspectral indices designed to quantify these harmonic dependencies while being intrinsically robust to instantaneous mixing. We derive the theoretical properties of these quantities and validate them through simulations of cubic nonlinearities. As a proof of concept, we apply the indices to empirical EEG recordings; the results reveal significant higher-order dependencies that elude standard analytical approaches. We further discuss how these indices can inform novel, personalized multi-site transcranial magnetic stimulation (mTMS) protocols by enabling the selective monitoring and modulation of specific multi-frequency network interactions.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a new family of antisymmetric cross-polyspectral indices for detecting genuine cross-frequency interactions in brain signals, robust to volume conduction artifacts, and demonstrates their utility on EEG data with implications for personalized mTMS protocols.
AI.motivation: Existing metrics cannot robustly characterize genuine multi-frequency interactions (where f_N = sum of f_i) while being immune to spurious zero-lag artifacts caused by volume conduction.
AI.method: The authors propose a general family of antisymmetric cross-polyspectral indices that are theoretically robust to instantaneous mixing, derived mathematically and validated through simulations of cubic nonlinearities.
AI.result: Simulations validated the method; empirical EEG analysis revealed significant higher-order harmonic dependencies missed by standard approaches.
AI.conclusion: These indices can inform novel, personalized multi-site transcranial magnetic stimulation (mTMS) protocols by enabling selective monitoring and modulation of specific multi-frequency network interactions.

title: TCRTransBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Bidirectional TCR-Peptide Sequence Generation
summary: T-cell receptor (TCR) interactions with antigenic peptides underpin adaptive immunity and are pivotal for personalized immunotherapy and vaccine development. Despite recent progress, computational modeling of TCR-peptide specificity remains challenging due to data scarcity, complex sequence dependencies, and the absence of standardized evaluation frameworks. To systematically address these issues, we introduce TCRTransBench, a comprehensive benchmark for bidirectional TCR-peptide sequence generation tasks. Specifically, we define two sequence-to-sequence (seq2seq) tasks: generating antigenic peptides from TCR sequences (TCR2PEP) and generating TCR sequences from antigenic peptides (PEP2TCR). Our framework provides a rigorously curated, MHC-free dataset comprising tens of thousands of validated TCR-peptide pairs, along with diverse evaluation metrics that integrate computational efficiency, sequence accuracy, and biological plausibility. Extensive benchmarking across representative neural architectures, including recurrent, convolutional, and transformer-based models, reveals key trade-offs among performance metrics, highlighting the effectiveness of transformers in capturing intricate biological interactions and the necessity of biologically informed evaluation criteria. TCRTransBench establishes standardized tasks, datasets, and evaluation protocols, laying a robust foundation for future computational advances in immunological sequence modeling and therapeutic protein design.

AI.tldr: TCRTransBench is a comprehensive benchmark for bidirectional TCR-peptide sequence generation, providing standardized datasets, tasks, and evaluation metrics to advance computational modeling of TCR-peptide specificity for immunotherapy and vaccine development.
AI.motivation: To address challenges in computational modeling of TCR-peptide specificity, including data scarcity, complex sequence dependencies, and lack of standardized evaluation frameworks.
AI.method: Defines two seq2seq tasks (TCR2PEP and PEP2TCR), provides a curated MHC-free dataset with tens of thousands of validated TCR-peptide pairs, and benchmarks representative neural architectures (RNN, CNN, transformers) using diverse evaluation metrics integrating computational efficiency, sequence accuracy, and biological plausibility.
AI.result: Transformers are effective in capturing intricate biological interactions; key trade-offs among performance metrics are revealed; biologically informed evaluation criteria are necessary.
AI.conclusion: TCRTransBench establishes standardized tasks, datasets, and evaluation protocols, laying a foundation for future computational advances in immunological sequence modeling and therapeutic protein design.

title: Think-Aloud Reshapes Automated Cognitive Model Discovery Beyond Behavior
summary: Computational cognitive models discovered using large language models have so far relied solely on behavioral data. However, it is well-known that models produced from the behavioral trajectory alone are typically under-determined. In this work, we explore the use of Think Aloud traces as an additional form of data constraint during automated model discovery. When applied to the domain of risky decision-making, we find that the models discovered with think-aloud achieve significantly improved predictive performance on held-out data. Additionally, we find that the discovered models belong to different structural classes than those discovered from behavior alone for the majority of participants (69.4\%), specifically, it shifts from Explicit comparator towards Integrated utility. These results suggest that process-level language data not only improve model fit, but also systematically reshape the structure of the discovered cognitive models, enabling the identification of mechanisms that are not recoverable from behavior alone.

AI.tldr: Using think-aloud language data as additional constraints improves AI-discovered cognitive models of decision-making, shifting model structures from Explicit comparator to Integrated utility for 69.4% of participants.
AI.motivation: Behavioral data alone under-determines cognitive models; think-aloud traces provide process-level information to better constrain model discovery.
AI.method: Applied large language models to discover cognitive models using both behavioral data and Think Aloud traces in risky decision-making tasks.
AI.result: Models incorporating think-aloud data achieved significantly better predictive performance on held-out data; 69.4% of participants' discovered models shifted from Explicit comparator to Integrated utility structures.
AI.conclusion: Process-level language data improves model fit and systematically reshapes cognitive model structure, enabling identification of mechanisms not recoverable from behavior alone.

title: Learning reveals invisible structure in low-rank RNNs
summary: Learning in neural systems arises from synaptic changes that reshape the representations underlying behavior. While low-rank recurrent neural networks (RNNs) have emerged as a powerful framework for linking connectivity to function, a theoretical understanding of their learning process remains elusive. Here, we extend the low-rank framework from activity to learning by deriving gradient-descent dynamics directly in a reduced overlap space. We formulate a closed-form, low-dimensional system of ODEs that governs learning in this space, exact for linear RNNs and asymptotically exact for nonlinear RNNs in the large-$N$ Gaussian limit. Central to our analysis is a distinction between two classes of overlaps: loss-visible overlaps, which fully determine network activity, output, and loss, and loss-invisible overlaps, which do not affect function but are required to describe learning. We illustrate the consequences of this decomposition through two phenomena. First, we show that learning can serve as a perturbation that exposes differences in connectivity between functionally equivalent networks. Second, we show that loss-invisible overlaps can act as memory variables that encode training history, and characterize the conditions under which this occurs. Finally, we present several testable predictions for biological learning experiments derived from our theory.

AI.tldr: This paper derives a low-dimensional ODE framework for learning dynamics in RNNs by decomposing synaptic overlaps into loss-visible and loss-invisible components, revealing how functionally equivalent networks can differ in connectivity and how training history is encoded.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between low-rank RNN connectivity and learning dynamics, providing a theoretical understanding of how gradient descent reshapes representations in neural networks.
AI.method: Extends low-rank framework from activity to learning by deriving gradient-descent dynamics in a reduced overlap space; formulates closed-form low-dimensional ODEs (exact for linear RNNs, asymptotically exact for nonlinear RNNs in large-N Gaussian limit); introduces decomposition into loss-visible and loss-invisible overlaps.
AI.result: Shows that: (1) learning can expose differences in connectivity between functionally equivalent networks, (2) loss-invisible overlaps act as memory variables encoding training history with characterized conditions.
AI.conclusion: Provides testable predictions for biological learning experiments and a theoretical framework for understanding learning dynamics beyond function-critical connectivity.

title: A Zero-Inflated Beta Mixture Model for Marginal Mediation Analysis with Compositional Microbiome Mediators
comment: 19 pages including references; 2 figures; Seungjun Ahn, Quran Wu: These authors contributed equally
summary: The role of the microbiome in disease pathogenesis is an emerging field with strong evidence suggesting that dysbiosis is associated with precancerous and cancerous states. Microbiome data present substantial challenges for causal mediation analysis due to sparsity, compositional constraints, and latent heterogeneity. To address these issues, we propose a zero-inflated beta mixture (ZIBM) method for mediation analysis with compositional microbiome mediators. The proposed method accommodates excess zeros through a zero-inflation component and captures heterogeneity in non-zero relative abundances using a beta mixture distribution. Within the potential-outcomes framework, the ZIBM provides estimates of marginal microbiome-mediated causal effects, and model parameters are estimated using an expectation-maximization algorithm. Simulation studies demonstrate that the ZIBM yields more accurate estimation and reliable inference under conditions commonly observed in microbiome data, compared with existing approaches. An application to a real microbiome study further illustrates its practical utility. These results indicate that the proposed method provides a more flexible and robust statistical framework for mediation analysis involving compositional microbiome data.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Zero-Inflated Beta Mixture (ZIBM) method for causal mediation analysis with compositional microbiome data, handling sparsity, zeros, and heterogeneity through a beta mixture distribution and EM algorithm, showing improved accuracy over existing methods.
AI.motivation: Microbiome data pose challenges for causal mediation analysis due to sparsity, compositional constraints, and latent heterogeneity, yet understanding microbiome-mediated effects in disease pathogenesis is important.
AI.method: Zero-inflated beta mixture (ZIBM) method with a zero-inflation component for excess zeros, a beta mixture distribution for heterogeneous non-zero abundances, EM algorithm for parameter estimation, and potential-outcomes framework for causal effects.
AI.result: Simulations show ZIBM yields more accurate estimation and reliable inference under microbiome data conditions compared to existing approaches; a real application demonstrates practical utility.
AI.conclusion: The ZIBM provides a more flexible and robust statistical framework for mediation analysis involving compositional microbiome data.

title: Loop Extrusion Reversal by Condensin Motor is Mediated by Catch Bonds
summary: Structural Maintenance Complexes (SMC) are energy consuming motors that are important in folding the genome by loop extrusion (LE) in all stages of the cell cycle. Single molecule magnetic tweezer pulling experiments have revealed that condensin, a member of the SMC family involved in mitosis, takes occasional backward steps, thus coughing up the gains in the length of the extruded loop. To reveal the mechanism of the forward and backward steps simultaneously, we developed a theory using the stochastic kinetic model and the scrunching mechanism for LE. The calculations quantitatively account for the measured force-dependent step size and dwell time distributions in both the directions. By postulating the existence of an intermediate state in the ATP-driven cycle that is poised to take a forward or a backward step, we predict that its lifetime increases as the external mechanical force increases till a critical value and subsequently decreases at higher forces. The surprising finding of lifetime increase in an active motor, at sub-piconewton forces, is the characteristic of catch bonds, known in force-induced rupture of several passive protein complexes. The identification of catch bond-like states in condensin not only expands our understanding of LE but also highlights the significance of mechanical forces in regulating genome organization.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a stochastic kinetic model of condensin loop extrusion that explains both forward and backward stepping behavior observed in magnetic tweezer experiments, revealing a catch-bond-like intermediate state whose lifetime increases with force up to a critical threshold.
AI.motivation: To understand the mechanism behind both forward and backward steps in condensin loop extrusion, particularly the puzzling backward "coughing" steps observed in single-molecule experiments.
AI.method: Stochastic kinetic model combined with the scrunching mechanism for loop extrusion to quantitatively analyze force-dependent step size and dwell time distributions.
AI.result: The model quantitatively accounts for measured step size and dwell time distributions. It predicts an intermediate state in the ATP cycle whose lifetime increases with force up to a critical value then decreases, characteristic of catch bonds.
AI.conclusion: Catch-bond-like states exist in condensin, expanding our understanding of loop extrusion and highlighting mechanical forces' role in regulating genome organization.

title: FairEnc: A Fair Vision-Language Model with Fair Vision and Text Encoders for Glaucoma Detection
summary: Automated glaucoma detection is critical for preventing irreversible vision loss and reducing the burden on healthcare systems. However, ensuring fairness across diverse patient populations remains a significant challenge. In this paper, we propose FairEnc, a fair pretraining method for vision-language models (VLMs) that enables simultaneous debiasing across multiple sensitive attributes. FairEnc jointly mitigates biases in both textual and visual modalities with respect to multiple sensitive attributes, including race, gender, ethnicity, and language. Specifically, for the textual encoder, we leverage a large language model to generate synthetic clinical descriptions with varied sensitive attributes while preserving disease semantics, and employ a contrastive alignment objective to encourage demographic-invariant representations. For the visual encoder, we propose a dual-level fairness strategy that combines mutual information regularization to reduce statistical dependence between learned features and demographic groups, with multi-discriminator adversarial debiasing. Comprehensive experiments on the publicly available Harvard-FairVLMed dataset demonstrate that FairEnc effectively reduces demographic disparity as measured by DPD and DEOdds while achieving strong diagnostic performance under both zero-shot and linear probing evaluations. Additional experiments on the private FairFundus dataset show that FairEnc consistently preserves fairness advantages under cross-domain and cross-modality settings and maintains diagnostic performance within a competitive range. These results highlight FairEnc's ability to generalize fairness under distribution shifts, supporting its potential for more equitable deployment in real-world clinical settings. Our codebase and synthetic clinical notes are available at https://github.com/Mohamed-Elhabebe/FairEnc

AI.tldr: FairEnc is a fair pretraining method for vision-language models that jointly debiases textual and visual encoders across multiple sensitive attributes (race, gender, ethnicity, language) using LLM-generated synthetic descriptions, contrastive alignment, mutual information regularization, and multi-discriminator adversarial debiasing, achieving reduced demographic disparity with strong diagnostic performance on glaucoma detection.
AI.motivation: Automated glaucoma detection is critical for preventing vision loss, but ensuring fairness across diverse patient populations (race, gender, ethnicity, language) remains a significant challenge that existing methods often fail to address jointly across multiple sensitive attributes and modalities.
AI.method: FairEnc uses: (1) LLM to generate synthetic clinical descriptions with varied sensitive attributes while preserving disease semantics; (2) contrastive alignment for demographic-invariant text representations; (3) mutual information regularization to reduce statistical dependence between visual features and demographics; (4) multi-discriminator adversarial debiasing for the visual encoder.
AI.result: On Harvard-FairVLMed, FairEnc reduces demographic disparity (DPD, DEOdds) while maintaining strong diagnostic performance in zero-shot and linear probing. On private FairFundus, it preserves fairness advantages under cross-domain/cross-modality settings with competitive diagnostic performance.
AI.conclusion: FairEnc generalizes fairness under distribution shifts, supporting its potential for more equitable deployment in real-world clinical settings for automated glaucoma detection.

title: Curated AI beats frontier LLMs at pharma asset discovery
summary: General-purpose LLMs with web search are increasingly used to scout the competitive landscape of pharmaceutical pipelines. We benchmark Gosset -- an AI platform with a chat interface backed by curated target-, modality-, and indication-level drug-asset annotations -- against four frontier systems with web access (Claude Opus 4.7, GPT 5.5, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Perplexity sonar-pro) on ten niche oncology/immunology targets where most of the pipeline lives in the long tail of preclinical and Asian-developed assets. All five systems receive the same natural-language query and the same JSON output schema. Across 10 targets Gosset returns 3.2x more verified drugs per query than the best frontier system, at perfect precision and 100% recall against the cross-system union of verified drugs. The same curated index is exposed as a Gosset MCP server that any frontier model can call as a tool, suggesting that each of these systems can close most of the recall gap by swapping generic web search for a curated index behind the same chat interface.

AI.tldr: Gosset, an AI platform with curated drug-asset annotations, outperforms four frontier LLMs (Claude Opus 4.7, GPT 5.5, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Perplexity sonar-pro) by 3.2x in verified drug recall on niche oncology/immunology targets, with perfect precision and 100% recall, and can be exposed as an MCP server for other models.
AI.motivation: To benchmark whether curated AI platforms (Gosset) outperform general-purpose LLMs with web search in scouting competitive pharmaceutical pipelines, especially for niche targets where most assets are preclinical or Asian-developed.
AI.method: Gosset is evaluated against four frontier systems (Claude Opus 4.7, GPT 5.5, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Perplexity sonar-pro) on ten niche oncology/immunology targets, using identical natural-language queries and JSON output schemas. Gosset's curated index is also exposed as an MCP server for other models.
AI.result: Gosset returns 3.2x more verified drugs per query than the best frontier system, with perfect precision and 100% recall against the cross-system union of verified drugs.
AI.conclusion: Frontier LLMs can close most of the recall gap by swapping generic web search for a curated index (like Gosset) behind the same chat interface, suggesting the bottleneck is the data source, not the model capability.

title: When Does Gene Regulatory Network Inference Break? A Controlled Diagnostic Study of Causal and Correlational Methods on Single-Cell Data
summary: Despite theoretical advantages, causal methods for Gene Regulatory Network (GRN) inference from single-cell RNA-seq data consistently fail to match or outperform correlation-based baselines in many realistic benchmarks, a persistent puzzle which casts doubt on the value of causality for this task. We argue that existing benchmarks are insufficiently controlled to answer this question because they evaluate on real or semi-real data where multiple pathologies co-occur, confounding failure modes, and obscuring the specific conditions under which different inference methods excel or fail. To address this gap, we introduce a controlled diagnostic framework that isolates seven biologically motivated pathologies (dropout, latent confounders, cell-type mixing, feedback loops, network density, sample size, and pseudotime drift) and measure how six representative methods spanning three inference paradigms degrade as each pathology intensifies. Across 6,120 controlled experiments, we find that causal methods genuinely dominate in clean and structurally favorable regimes, but specific pathologies (notably dropout and latent confounders) selectively neutralize their advantages. We further introduce an error-type decomposition that reveals methods with similar aggregate accuracy commit qualitatively different errors. To probe whether single-pathology effects persist when multiple stressors co-occur, we perform an interaction sweep over the three most impactful pathologies and find that their joint effects are sub-additive, while also exposing density-conditional cross-overs invisible to single-dial analysis. Our findings offer a nuanced understanding of when and why different methods succeed or fail for GRN inference, providing actionable insights for method development and practical guidance for practitioners.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a controlled diagnostic framework to evaluate causal vs. correlation-based GRN inference methods across 6,120 experiments with seven isolated pathologies, finding causal methods excel in clean regimes but specific pathologies (dropout, latent confounders) neutralize their advantages, and that joint pathology effects are sub-additive with density-conditional cross-overs.
AI.motivation: Causal methods for GRN inference consistently fail to outperform correlation-based baselines in benchmarks, but existing evaluations are insufficiently controlled due to multiple co-occurring pathologies, making it unclear where and why different methods succeed or fail.
AI.method: A controlled diagnostic framework isolates seven biologically motivated pathologies (dropout, latent confounders, cell-type mixing, feedback loops, network density, sample size, pseudotime drift) and evaluates six representative methods across three inference paradigms in 6,120 controlled experiments, with an error-type decomposition and interaction sweep over the three most impactful pathologies.
AI.result: Causal methods dominate in clean regimes; dropout and latent confounders selectively neutralize causal advantages; methods with similar aggregate accuracy make qualitatively different errors; joint pathology effects are sub-additive; density-conditional cross-overs appear under co-occurring pathologies that single-dial analysis misses.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide a nuanced understanding of when and why different GRN inference methods succeed or fail, offering actionable insights for method development and practical guidance for practitioners.
